title: GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease
Identify key diagnostic features of GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease
Apply clinical reasoning to GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease management
Recognize common pitfalls in GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease

sections.title: Overview of GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease
sections.content:
**GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease**

GERD pathophysiology, alarm features, PPI therapy, H. pylori testing and treatment, NSAID ulcers

**Key Points:**
• GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis
• Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy
• PPI therapy: 8-week trial for typical GERD; step down to H2RA if controlled

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis
• Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• PPI therapy: 8-week trial for typical GERD; step down to H2RA if controlled
• H. pylori: urea breath test or stool antigen for non-invasive diagnosis; biopsy-based if endoscopy done
• Triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin (14 days); Bismuth quadruple if penicillin allergic

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease:**

• PPI therapy: 8-week trial for typical GERD; step down to H2RA if controlled
• H. pylori: urea breath test or stool antigen for non-invasive diagnosis; biopsy-based if endoscopy done
• Triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin (14 days); Bismuth quadruple if penicillin allergic

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease?

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases
GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis
options.explanation: GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis

correct.reinforcement: Correct! GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis

coachDx.misconception: While typically self-resolving without treatment may seem plausible, it does not match the clinical scenario described.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis from Typically self-resolving without treatment.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves GERD: transient LES relaxation → acid reflux → esophagitis.

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with GERD & Peptic Ulcer Disease. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy
options.explanation: Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy

coachDx.misconception: While pathognomonic for an unrelated condition may seem plausible, it does not match the clinical scenario described.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia) → endoscopy from Pathognomonic for an unrelated condition.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old with chronic diarrhea has anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA of 120 U/mL (normal <20) and total IgA of 250 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat tTG-IgA in 3 months to confirm persistence
options.explanation: A tTG-IgA of 120 U/mL (6× upper limit) has very high positive predictive value. Repeating delays diagnosis unnecessarily; proceed to biopsy.

options.text: Start a gluten-free diet immediately and recheck tTG in 6 months
options.explanation: Starting a GFD before biopsy confirmation can normalize histology, preventing definitive diagnosis. Biopsy is the gold standard and should be obtained first.

options.text: Check HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genotyping to confirm diagnosis
options.explanation: HLA-DQ2/DQ8 has high negative predictive value (absence essentially rules out celiac) but ~30% of the general population carries these alleles, so positive results alone do not confirm disease.

options.text: Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies while patient remains on gluten-containing diet
options.explanation: Per ACG guidelines, positive tTG-IgA with adequate total IgA should be confirmed with duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy (Marsh classification). Critically, the patient must remain on a gluten-containing diet — eliminating gluten before biopsy can cause false-negative histology.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies while patient remains on gluten-containing diet.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies while patient remains on gluten-containing diet

coachDx.misconception: A tTG-IgA of 120 U/mL (6× upper limit) has very high positive predictive value. Repeating delays diagnosis unnecessarily; proceed to biopsy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies while patient remains on gluten-containin from Repeat tTG-IgA in 3 months to confirm persistence.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies while patient remains on gluten-containing diet.
